findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (or in test_decomp_shape_criterion.py's AC2 scope) that `EXPECTED_ROUTING` in `test_threshold_recalibration.py` must be extended with `'decomp-shape': (0.95, 'advisory')` so the full-table regression pin stays green.
evidence: test_threshold_recalibration.py:133-136: `def test_full_routing_table_matches_pinned_expectation(): ... assert actual == EXPECTED_ROUTING`
EXPECTED_ROUTING dict (lines 33-72) enumerates every current built-in criterion but not decomp-shape
Plan Files list includes test_threshold_recalibration.py but no AC or description specifies what change is made to it
Plan AC2 only references test_decomp_shape_criterion.py for unit tests
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` is named in the plan's Files list but the plan provides no AC or description of what edit is required. The file contains `test_full_routing_table_matches_pinned_expectation()` (line 133-136) which asserts `actual == EXPECTED_ROUTING` — a full-table pin over ALL built-in criteria. Adding decomp-shape to CANONICAL_LLM and criteria_routing.json will cause this test to fail because `EXPECTED_ROUTING` does not include `decomp-shape`. The plan's AC1 proves command and AC2 unit-test commands do not reference this file.
scenarios: Developer adds decomp-shape to CANONICAL_LLM + criteria_routing.json per the plan and runs the test suite; test_full_routing_table_matches_pinned_expectation fails because EXPECTED_ROUTING lacks the new entry, breaking CI.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"decomp-shape": (0.95, "advisory")` to the EXPECTED_ROUTING dict in test_threshold_recalibration.py as part of this story's landing.

findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` is listed in the Scope/Files section as a named deliverable but no AC verifies its content, behavior, or that it passes — the only test-proof command in AC2 runs `pytest tests/unit/test_decomp_shape_criterion.py -q`, leaving this file as uncovered work.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2's proof to `pytest tests/unit/test_decomp_shape_criterion.py tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py -q` and add one sentence describing what recalibration logic the latter file tests (e.g., that adding decomp-shape does not shift existing criterion thresholds unexpectedly).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add coverage of `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` to AC2 (or a new AC): state what threshold-recalibration logic it must exercise and include it in the pytest proof command (e.g. `pytest tests/unit/test_decomp_shape_criterion.py tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py -q`).
evidence: Scope names `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` as a target file.
AC2 proof is `pytest tests/unit/test_decomp_shape_criterion.py -q` — does not include or mention test_threshold_recalibration.py.
No other AC references this file or describes its required coverage.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py` is listed in the Scope file manifest but no Acceptance Criterion covers what it must verify or that it passes.
findings.location: Scope (file list: `tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py`); AC2 (proof cites only `pytest tests/unit/test_decomp_shape_criterion.py -q`)
scenarios: Implementer creates the file but with vacuous or incorrect tests — no AC will catch it.
Reviewer marking ACs complete has no criterion to evaluate `test_threshold_recalibration.py` against.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2's description to include: 'threshold-recalibration interactions (e.g. that adding decomp-shape does not disturb existing threshold computations)' and update the proof to `pytest tests/unit/test_decomp_shape_criterion.py tests/unit/test_threshold_recalibration.py -q`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a grep-based proving command for AC5, e.g. `grep -q 'decomp-shape' docs/plan-review-gate.md && grep -q 'order-stabilit' docs/plan-review-gate.md && echo OK`.
evidence: AC5 proof text: 'the section names `decomp-shape` and the E6 order-stability prerequisite' — no command given
All other ACs (AC1–AC4) supply explicit shell/pytest commands; AC5 is prose-only
Verify-command defect type 6: no structured assertion on the artifact
findings.finding: AC5 (promotion-gate documentation) has no concrete proving command; it says the section 'names `decomp-shape` and the E6 order-stability prerequisite' but provides no grep or search command that verifiably asserts these strings are present in `docs/plan-review-gate.md`.
scenarios: A reviewer cannot deterministically confirm AC5 is met without a command; the section could be present but omit the E6 prerequisite language, and no check catches it.
findings.suggested_fix: grep -q 'decomp-shape' docs/plan-review-gate.md && grep -qE 'fleiss_kappa|order.stabilit' docs/plan-review-gate.md && echo OK

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the rubric prompt instructs the model to handle the producer-sibling visibility gap: either (a) note that only id+title is available for out-of-bin siblings and instruct conservative flagging when a consumed artifact's producer is not visible whole, or (b) consider augmenting the roster with artifact-hint fields (e.g. output files/events from each child's description) when feasible.
evidence: pass1.py:154: `roster = '\n'.join(f'- {c.get("ticket_id")}: {c.get("title", "")}' for c in ctx.children)` — roster is id+title only, not full child descriptions
passes.py:407: `## Complete sibling roster (for absence cross-check)\n{sibling_roster}` — the model only receives name-only entries for siblings outside the current bin

Plan states: 'a child NAMES/CONSUMES an artifact a SIBLING produces (its output file, schema, event, symbol), yet there is NO ordering dependency' — detecting this requires knowing what the sibling produces, which is not in the roster
findings.finding: The consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge smell requires knowing what artifact a SIBLING child PRODUCES — but the sibling roster fed to pass1_container contains only id+title, not full sibling descriptions. A child outside the current bin is not visible in full text. The model can see the consumer child whole but cannot reliably identify the producer's specific artifact names from a name-only roster entry, making the detection of cross-child missing-edge smells unreliable when the producer and consumer land in different bins.
findings.location: What section / 'consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge' smell; passes.py:403-409 (sibling_roster construction at pass1.py:154)
scenarios: Child A (in bin 1) produces a schema file 'auth_events.proto'; child B (in bin 2) references 'auth_events.proto' in its description. When bin 2 is evaluated, the roster shows '- <A-id>: Auth event schema work' but not A's description — the model cannot reliably infer A produces that specific artifact and may miss the missing dependency edge.
Two children are packed into separate bins due to size; the consumed-artifact smell spans them — it fires only if the model guesses from the title that one produces an artifact the other consumes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `decomp-shape` gets access to the FULL child set (not just its packed bin) — either by running once per container (not per bin) at a dedicated seam, or by enriching the sibling roster with descriptions, or by adding a single-pass whole-container call path for shape-only criteria.
evidence: Plan states: 'Adding `decomp-shape` to `CONTAINER_CRITERIA` fans it out through that same seam with per-criterion + per-child attribution; nothing else changes.'
pass1.py:154: `roster = '\n'.join(f'- {c.get("ticket_id")}: {c.get("title", "")}' for c in ctx.children)` — roster is id+title only, no descriptions.
sizing.py:48-76: `pack_container_bins` distributes children across multiple bins when the token budget requires it; each bin is an independent pass1_container call.
passes.py:385-388: each pass1_container call's `children_block` contains only the bin's children, not the full sibling set.
passes.py:357 docstring: 'Run ALL container criteria (G3+G4) for a parent + a BIN of one-or-more WHOLE children' — explicitly per-BIN, not per-container.
findings.finding: The `decomp-shape` criterion is a WHOLE-decomposition partition-axis audit — it needs to classify the ENTIRE child set's partition axis to detect a layer-cake split — but the existing container seam is per-BIN: `pack_container_bins` splits children across bins and each `pass1_container` call receives only its bin's children plus a lightweight id+title sibling roster. For a container with enough children to span multiple bins, no single call sees the full child partition. The plan asserts it 'hands the finder parent + whole children + the complete sibling roster' but the sibling roster is `id: title` only (pass1.py:154), not the full child descriptions needed to classify the decomposition axis.
findings.location: What section — 'Mechanism seam (cited)' and 'Adding decomp-shape to CONTAINER_CRITERIA fans it out through that same seam'
scenarios: A container with 6 children packed into 3 bins of 2: a layer-cake split (DB child, API child, UI child, ...) would be seen as 2 children per call — not enough context to classify the partition axis as horizontal/vertical.

The consumed-artifact smell requires knowing what artifact sibling S1 produces; S1's description is in a different bin's children_block and is NOT in the sibling roster, so the model in S2's bin call cannot read S1's artifact definition.

findings.finding: The `decomp-shape` criterion is a WHOLE-decomposition partition-axis audit, but the existing container seam evaluates criteria per-BIN — each `pass1_container` call receives only its packed children as full text; other siblings appear in the roster as name+id strings only. For layer-cake detection (requires seeing ALL children's partition axes) and consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge detection (requires seeing both producer and consumer children's full text), multi-bin fan-outs silently reduce the evidence available to the model. The plan describes the seam as providing 'parent + whole children + the complete sibling roster' but in reality the other-bin children are only available as thin names — a false-negative risk the plan does not acknowledge or mitigate.
findings.location: What §Mechanism seam; pass1.py:166 (pack_container_bins); passes.py:357-455 (pass1_container); plan phrase 'hands the finder parent + whole children + the complete sibling roster'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC1 proving command to assert `e['applies_at']['scope'] == ['container']` (and optionally `e['block_threshold'] == 0.95`) so all plan-specified routing-entry fields are verified.
evidence: Plan specifies: `applies_at.scope:["container"]` as a required routing entry field
AC1 command checks: `e['exec']=='AGENT' and e['facet']=='container' and e['default_posture']=='advisory'` — no `applies_at` assertion
Verify-command defect type 4: fixture does not fully exercise the claim
findings.finding: AC1's proving command does not assert `applies_at.scope == ['container']`, which the plan explicitly requires as a field in the routing entry (`applies_at.scope:["container"]`). This is a verify-command defect: the AC can pass even if that field is missing or wrong, leaving a registration invariant unverified.
scenarios: A routing entry is committed without `applies_at.scope` (or with wrong scope like `["leaf"]`), AC1 still passes, but the criterion silently fails to gate at the container fan-out level.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC1 one-liner: `assert e['applies_at']['scope']==['container'] and e.get('block_threshold')==0.95;`

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean, improve, sufficient
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile R3's E2 gate status with the parent epic's INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE: either (a) run E2 (d125) for classes 2/3 and record the result before landing decomp-shape, or (b) formally close/defer R3 with the E2-gate failure recorded per the parent's 'no detection-bearing recommendation implemented without its eval gate' AC — the 'permanently rejected on cost' reclassification is not an authorized substitution under the parent's gate terms.
evidence: Parent epic 6982 integration record: 'R3=3a27 (decomposition-shape, advisory-only, gated E2+E6)'
Parent epic 6982 AC #3: 'no detection-bearing recommendation implemented without its eval gate'

Parent epic 6982 Success Criteria: 'Every implemented recommendation has a committed docs/experiments/ writeup showing its gate row passed before implementation; every rejected/deferred recommendation has the failing result recorded'
Parent epic 6982 INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE (Context): 'if still not clearly passing, every dependent R-story closes deferred with the results recorded — ambiguity never rounds up to a pass'
E2 ticket (d125) confirms R3's gate dependency: 'R3 (3a27) result gate — layer-cake + consumed-artifact-ordering detection (classes 2, 3) is judged on E2's class-2/3 mutants'
R3 Scope OUT: 'the E2/E3 mutant corpus (permanently rejected on cost); sanity uses hand-authored fixtures' — E2 is not closed/deferred with results recorded; it is in_progress
findings.finding: R3 ships a detection-bearing criterion while unilaterally reclassifying its parent-designated E2 eval gate as 'permanently rejected on cost' — substituting hand-authored sanity fixtures — without invoking the parent's INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE (which requires closing/deferring with results recorded when a gate cannot be run or fails, not substituting a lighter fixture and shipping). The parent's AC #3 states unconditionally 'no detection-bearing recommendation implemented without its eval gate,' and the parent's integration record designates E2 as one of R3's two required gates.
findings.location: R3 Scope section ('OUT: ... the E2/E3 mutant corpus (permanently rejected on cost); sanity uses hand-authored fixtures') vs. parent epic 6982 AC #3 and integration record comment ('R3=3a27 ... gated E2+E6')
scenarios: An implementer executes R3 as written, ships decomp-shape without E2 validation, and the parent epic AC #3 ('no detection-bearing recommendation implemented without its eval gate') is violated.
A reviewer auditing epic 6982's AC compliance finds R3 landed without its E2 gate and without a formally recorded INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE deferral — the parent's integrity guarantee is broken.
decomp-shape has a high false-positive rate that would have been caught by E2's seeded-defect discrimination test (≥7/10 mutants flagged, ≤1/10 controls flagged), but ships unvalidated into dogfood.
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) coordinate with E2 (d125, currently in_progress) to run its class-2/3 seeded-defect benchmark against decomp-shape before landing R3; or (b) add a plan section explicitly invoking the parent's INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE for E2 (record the cost constraint as the reason E2 cannot be run for R3, commit the non-result, and either close/defer R3 or — if the operator approves an exception — document it as an explicit operator override of the gate rule with the rationale recorded). The hand-authored sanity fixtures in AC #3 are a useful calibration artifact but do not satisfy the parent's eval-gate requirement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC3's recall requirement: either change '(target 1.0)' to '(required: 1.0; AC fails if <1.0)' or explicitly state that partial recall is acceptable with a documented note.
evidence: AC3 text: 'the criterion FIRES on both positives' (binary), but also 'records recall over the positives (target 1.0)' — the word 'target' introduces softness
'any negative fire is ADVISORY (never blocks), recorded as a promotion-gate FP signal' — negatives are explicitly soft; positives should be hard but 'target' blurs the line
findings.finding: AC3's recall gate is stated as '(target 1.0)' — ambiguous whether this is a hard PASS/FAIL requirement or a recorded aspiration. If the criterion fires on only 1 of 2 positive fixtures, it is unclear whether AC3 is considered failed.
scenarios: Criterion fires on only the consumed-artifact fixture but misses the layer-cake fixture; 'target 1.0' is not met but executor argues the AC is 'passed' since recall was recorded.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(target 1.0)' with '(required: both must fire; AC3 FAILS if either positive is a miss)' to make the gate binary and unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the conflict between R3's 'advisory shipping is unaffected' claim and the parent's INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE applied to E6's failed gate: either (a) document an explicit operator exception granting advisory shipping despite E6 failure (updating the parent if the rule is being relaxed), or (b) close/defer R3 per the parent's rule and record E6's failure as the reason.
evidence: Parent epic 6982 INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE: 'an experiment that fails its own validity gate ... gets at most ONE documented re-run with changed method; if still not clearly passing, every dependent R-story closes deferred with the results recorded — ambiguity never rounds up to a pass'
Parent epic 6982 integration record: 'R3=3a27 (decomposition-shape, advisory-only, gated E2+E6)' — E6 is a gate, not just a blocking-promotion gate

R3 What: 'E6's order-shuffle reliability FAILED (fleiss_kappa=0.55 < 0.6 floor, raw=0.7 < 0.8; e6_summary.json), which is a declared R3 PREREQUISITE FAILURE ... Advisory shipping is unaffected — a non-blocking coaching nudge is safe under judge instability'

The parent's INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE applies to E6 as a gate for R3, not only to blocking-promotion gating — the rule says 'every dependent R-story closes deferred', not 'every dependent R-story's blocking-promotion is deferred'
findings.finding: R3 treats E6's FAILED result (fleiss_kappa=0.55 < 0.6 floor) as only deferring blocking-promotion while shipping advisory, but the parent's INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE says a failed prerequisite gate means the dependent R-story 'closes deferred with the results recorded' — it does not authorize a partial ship ('advisory only, promotion deferred'). The parent designates E6 as a gate for R3; a failed gate has one authorized outcome under the parent: close/defer with the failing result recorded.
findings.location: R3 What section ('E6's order-shuffle reliability FAILED ... which is a declared R3 PREREQUISITE FAILURE ... Advisory shipping is unaffected') vs. parent epic 6982 INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE (Context) and integration record ('gated E2+E6')
scenarios: A reviewer applying the parent's INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE to E6's failure concludes R3 must close/defer, but R3 ships advisory instead — parent rule is violated.

An order-sensitive judge produces inconsistent advisory findings that confuse dogfood users, exactly the risk E6 was gating against, but decomp-shape shipped because its advisory posture was treated as exempt from the gate.
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is that the INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE applies differently to advisory-only criteria (i.e., judge order-instability only disqualifies blocking verdicts, not advisory coaching nudges), update the parent epic's INCONCLUSIVE-GATE RULE or add an operator-attested exception note to R3 that the parent explicitly endorses. The current leaf text asserts this exception without parent authorization.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the closed value vocabulary for both `has_container_decomposition` and `decomposition_shape_sound` sub-answers (e.g. `{yes|no}` or `{yes|no|insufficient}`) in the plan's 'What' section, co-located with the sub-answer description, matching the pattern from `asserted-capability` and `removal-rationale`.
evidence: Plan says: 'has_container_decomposition (GATE — parent has >=2 children forming a decomposition; `no` -> not-applicable -> PASS)' and 'decomposition_shape_sound (`no` when a layer-cake partition OR a consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge is present — the finding)' — neither defines the full legal value set.

plan_review_asserted_capability.md lines 53-58 shows the precedent: 'asserts_named_module_capability {yes|no|insufficient}' and 'asserted_capability_grounded {yes|no|insufficient}' co-located in the rubric body.
plan_review_removal_rationale.md lines 41-46 shows same pattern: '{yes|no|insufficient}' for both sub-answers.

passes.py lines 148-160 shows the existing completion sub-answers rely on named constants (COMPLETION_CONTAINMENT, COMPLETION_LAYER) whose tuples define the closed vocabulary and drive coercion logic — decomp-shape has no analogous vocabulary definition.
ABSENCE: no vocabulary constants or enum list for decomp-shape sub-answers appear anywhere in the plan text.
findings.finding: The value vocabulary (closed enum) for the two criterion-local checklist sub-answers `has_container_decomposition` and `decomposition_shape_sound` is never defined anywhere in the plan. Analogous criteria in the codebase (`asserted-capability` and `removal-rationale`) always co-locate the legal token values — e.g. `{yes|no|insufficient}` — in the rubric body text; the model self-reports these and the contract relies on a known closed set for coercion. The plan says 'gated-then-detected' but never names what token values `has_container_decomposition` (gate: yes/no? yes/no/na?) or `decomposition_shape_sound` (no/yes? no/yes/insufficient?) should emit, so the rubric author and the test author have no vocabulary anchor.
scenarios: A rubric author writing plan_review_decomp_shape.md has no spec for what tokens to instruct the model to emit — they must invent a vocabulary, creating a contract mismatch with whatever the unit test asserts.

A model emitting 'n/a' or 'not_applicable' for the gate sub-answer instead of 'no' would pass the structured contract but misroute the criterion; with no coercion rule specified, the gate is silently broken.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' section: 'has_container_decomposition {yes|no} — the GATE; no → not-applicable → PASS. decomposition_shape_sound {yes|no} — only meaningful when gated in; no when a layer-cake partition OR consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge is detected.' Mirror the {yes|no|insufficient} abstain option from peer criteria if partial observability applies.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/parity.py` to the Files list and specify that `parity.CONTAINER_CRITERIA` at line 167 must be updated to include `decomp-shape` (or refactored to import from pass1) so attribution accuracy scoring covers the new criterion.
evidence: Plan Files list does not include src/rebar/llm/parity.py
parity.py:167: `CONTAINER_CRITERIA = ('G3', 'G4')` — local constant, not imported from pass1
parity.py:184: `if r.gold_criterion in CONTAINER_CRITERIA` — uses the local constant for attribution scoring
pass1.py:43: `CONTAINER_CRITERIA = ('G3', 'G4')` — the separate constant the plan proposes to extend
findings.finding: src/rebar/llm/parity.py defines its own local `CONTAINER_CRITERIA = ('G3', 'G4')` constant at line 167 that is NOT imported from pass1.py. The plan does not list parity.py in its Files scope. Adding decomp-shape to pass1.CONTAINER_CRITERIA without also updating parity.CONTAINER_CRITERIA means decomp-shape container findings will be silently excluded from the attribution_accuracy() scoring in the parity harness — a divergence between the two constants that compounds if the parity harness is run.
scenarios: A decomp-shape container finding is flagged during a parity run; attribution_accuracy() does not count it because decomp-shape is not in parity.CONTAINER_CRITERIA, producing a silently incomplete attribution score.
A future caller references parity.CONTAINER_CRITERIA expecting the full container set and misses decomp-shape.
findings.suggested_fix: Either import CONTAINER_CRITERIA from pass1 in parity.py (replacing the local constant), or add 'decomp-shape' to parity.CONTAINER_CRITERIA alongside the pass1 change. Prefer the import to eliminate the dual-maintenance hazard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/parity.py` to the Scope/Files list and specify that `parity.CONTAINER_CRITERIA` must be updated (or replaced with an import from `pass1.py`) when `decomp-shape` is added to `pass1.CONTAINER_CRITERIA`.
evidence: parity.py:167: `CONTAINER_CRITERIA = ('G3', 'G4')` — a local tuple, no import from pass1.
parity.py:184: `if r.gold_criterion in CONTAINER_CRITERIA and r.decision == 'block' and r.pred_criterion` — drives attribution_accuracy().
Plan's Files list: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py`, `.../criteria_routing.json`, `.../pass1.py`, etc. — `src/rebar/llm/parity.py` is absent.
ABSENCE: no mention of `parity.py` anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: `parity.py:167` defines its own local `CONTAINER_CRITERIA = ('G3', 'G4')` constant that is NOT imported from `pass1.py`. The plan says 'adding `decomp-shape` to `CONTAINER_CRITERIA`' but only specifies updating `pass1.CONTAINER_CRITERIA`; `parity.CONTAINER_CRITERIA` — which gates the G3/G4 attribution-accuracy scoring in `container_fidelity_report` and `attribution_accuracy` — is never mentioned. Adding `decomp-shape` to `pass1.CONTAINER_CRITERIA` without updating `parity.py` silently excludes it from attribution-accuracy tracking.
findings.location: What section — 'Mechanism seam (cited)' and Scope/Files list
scenarios: A future container fidelity eval (S4/S5) runs `container_fidelity_report`; decomp-shape findings are never counted in `attribution_accuracy`, making the metric silently inaccurate if decomp-shape fires alongside G3/G4.

The eval_solver container-arm test exercises `pass1_container` with decomp-shape, but the parity gate still treats the criterion set as {G3, G4} — attribution scoring for decomp-shape is structurally dead.
findings.suggested_fix: In `parity.py`, replace the local `CONTAINER_CRITERIA = ('G3', 'G4')` with `from rebar.llm.plan_review.pass1 import CONTAINER_CRITERIA` to make it a single source of truth.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the eval_solver container-arm LLM calls: define what happens on timeout/API error per fixture (fail-fast with non-zero exit vs. record error row), and add an atomic write or post-run validation step so decomp_shape_sanity.json is never partially committed.
evidence: 'This story extends that arm to run a container criterion via pass1_container when the fixture carries a children: list — a small, contained enhancement'
AC3 proof: 'docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/decomp_shape_sanity.json preserves the raw per-case rebar criteria eval decomp-shape --output json output'
No mention of retry, timeout handling, or partial-write guard for the new eval_solver LLM call path
findings.finding: The eval_solver container-arm extension introduces a new LLM call path and writes decomp_shape_sanity.json, but the plan specifies no error handling for LLM call failures (timeout, API error, partial response) in this arm — a partially written result file could silently satisfy the AC proof-of-file check while containing incomplete data.
findings.location: What section — eval_solver container-arm extension; AC3 (decomp_shape_sanity.json committed result)
scenarios: LLM API times out mid-run; first 4 of 8 fixture cases complete and are written; the JSON file exists and passes the file-presence proof but records only partial recall — the committed sanity result is misleading.
Transient API error on a positive fixture causes that case to error rather than fire, lowering the observed recall below 1.0 without flagging the cause.
findings.suggested_fix: In the eval_solver extension spec, add: (a) per-fixture try/except that records an explicit `{"status": "error", "reason": ...}` row rather than silently omitting the case; (b) write the JSON atomically (write to .tmp, rename on success) or add a post-run completeness assertion (all fixture ids present) before the file is committed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'd8a5' citation in AC5: either correct the ticket/story id to the actual promotion-gate protocol reference visible in the repo, or remove the citation and point directly to the `docs/plan-review-gate.md` §'Standing per-criterion effectiveness recorder + advisory->blocking promotion gate' section that already documents the gate.
evidence: Plan text: 'The advisory->blocking promotion gate is documented, citing d8a5's protocol in docs/plan-review-gate.md'
Repo-wide search for 'd8a5' returns zero hits in any source file, doc, or data file

docs/plan-review-gate.md §'Standing per-criterion effectiveness recorder + advisory->blocking promotion gate' (lines 621–674) describes the promotion gate but never references any 'd8a5' story or protocol identifier
ABSENCE: no ticket, ADR, doc section, or code comment containing 'd8a5' was found
findings.finding: AC5 cites 'd8a5's protocol' as the named promotion-gate protocol in docs/plan-review-gate.md, but no story, ticket, or document with the id 'd8a5' exists anywhere in the repository. A full-repo search returns zero matches.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5: 'citing d8a5's protocol in docs/plan-review-gate.md §"Standing per-criterion effectiveness recorder + advisory->blocking promotion gate"'
scenarios: The implementer searches for the 'd8a5' protocol to understand what the AC demands and finds nothing — the acceptance criterion is unverifiable as written.
A reviewer auditing the committed docs/plan-review-gate.md cannot confirm the 'd8a5' citation is present, so the AC cannot be attested.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'citing d8a5's protocol' with 'per the protocol in docs/plan-review-gate.md §"Standing per-criterion effectiveness recorder + advisory->blocking promotion gate"' — the section already exists and fully documents the gate; no external story reference is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the passes.py line-number citations in the Mechanism seam section: valid_ids is at line 382, rubric at line 389, and the attribution-filter guard (the loop that enforces valid_ids) is at line 420 — update the three cited line numbers accordingly.
evidence: passes.py line 382: `valid_ids = [c['id'] for c in criteria]` (plan claims line 381)
passes.py line 389: `rubric = '\n\n'.join(_criterion_block(c) for c in criteria)` (plan claims line 387)
passes.py line 420: `crit = [c for c in (f.get('criteria') or []) if c in valid_ids]` — the actual attribution filter (plan claims line 428)
passes.py line 428: a comment '# a multi-child finding left unattributed stays bin-level (None), not mis-assigned.' — not the valid_ids guard
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'validates model self-reported attribution against valid_ids (passes.py:381, 428)' and 'rubric = ... passes.py:387'. Reading the file: valid_ids is defined at passes.py:382 (not 381), rubric is at passes.py:389 (not 387), and line 428 is a comment about child-id word-boundary matching — the actual valid_ids attribution-filter is at passes.py:420. These are off-by-1 to off-by-8 line citations.
findings.location: Why §, 'Mechanism seam (cited)': 'validates model self-reported attribution against valid_ids (passes.py:381, 428)' and 'rubric = "\n\n".join(_criterion_block(c) ...), passes.py:387'
scenarios: An implementer following the plan to add decomp-shape to the validated set uses the stated line numbers and reaches incorrect code locations, potentially misunderstanding the validation mechanism.
A code reviewer checking that decomp-shape's id appears in valid_ids uses the cited line numbers and lands on comment text or the wrong variable definition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the container-arm fixture schema (which key provides parent_plan, the structure of `children` entries, and how sibling_roster is derived) in the eval fixture file or the 'Bounded sanity harness' section so the unit test and sanity fixture are written to the same contract.
evidence: Plan: 'This story extends that arm to run a container criterion via pass1_container when the fixture carries a `children:` list'
Plan names fixture file `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-decomp-shape.eval.yaml` but does not specify its schema beyond 'hand-authored fixtures'
eval_solver.py:110: `plan = case.get('input') or ''` — the existing inline-text arm uses 'input'; the container arm presumably uses 'input' as parent_plan too, but this is not stated
passes.py:357: `pass1_container(..., parent_plan, children, criteria, sibling_roster)` — four distinct inputs needed; fixture must supply them all
findings.finding: The plan specifies that eval_solver will be extended to run a container criterion via pass1_container when the fixture carries a `children:` list, but does not specify the fixture schema — specifically, which fixture key provides `parent_plan`, how `sibling_roster` is populated (empty string? derived from the children list?), and what structure `children` entries must have (ticket_id, title, description keys). The AC proof assertion only checks that `rebar criteria eval decomp-shape --output json` produces output; without a documented fixture schema, the eval fixture author (and the unit test author) has no contract to test against.
findings.location: What section ('Bounded sanity harness') / eval_solver.py:91-115 / AC bullet 3
scenarios: A developer writes the eval fixture using 'plan' as the parent key instead of 'input' — the extended arm silently passes an empty parent_plan to pass1_container, producing misleading sanity results.
The unit test for the eval_solver container arm uses a different fixture shape than the live sanity run — the test passes but the live run fails, undermining the harness.
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that the fixture format is: `input:` → parent_plan text, `children:` → list of `{ticket_id, title, description}` dicts, and `sibling_roster` derived automatically as the id+title join of the children list (or optionally overridden by a `sibling_roster:` key).

findings.finding: AC #1's proving command will raise ImportError before it can assert anything: `from rebar.llm.plan_review import registry as r, json` attempts to import the standard-library `json` module from the `rebar.llm.plan_review` package namespace, which does not export it — this is not a valid multi-name import from a package.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the first line of the python -c block with:
`from rebar.llm.plan_review import registry as r; import json`

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
